schools.summary: Ellicott renders the verse the superscription of the section (to v.19), noting the writer starts 'ab ovo'; Keil and Delitzsch preface it with the genealogy — Nadab and Abihu died childless, so only Eleazar and Ithamar were priests.

Matthew Henry: 'divisions' means not variance but the distribution of work by God, the God of order; and the eighth course (Abijah) proves the courses lasted to the second temple, since Zacharias belonged to it (Luke 1:5).
Wesley glosses 'divisions' as the branches of the family; Clarke lays out the chapter's plan — Eleazar and Ithamar into twenty-four courses (vv.1-19), the rest of Levi ordered from v.20.
Gill takes 'divisions' as simply the classes or courses that follow.

themes: the divisions of the sons of Aaron
courses enduring to the second temple

schools.summary: Ellicott and Keil ground the notice in Lev. 10:1-2 (the 'strange fire') and Num. 3:4, reading 'had no children' as 'sons had not been born to them,' so the priesthood necessarily fell to the two houses.
Clarke traces the office through Eleazar's succession and its later return to Ithamar's line under Eli; Wesley: the two survivors were the only ones to whom the office was committed.
Gill notes simply that Nadab and Abihu died before their father, in his lifetime.

themes: the office through Eleazar and Ithamar

schools.summary: Ellicott parses 'Zadok and Ahimelech' into the subject — 'David, with Zadok and Ahimelech, divided them' — the two heads assisting the king 'according to their official class'; Keil reads 'Ahimelech the son of Abiathar' as the plain text stands.
Wesley: the division allotted each course its turns of holy ministration; Clarke holds Ahimelech here is Abiathar himself, who also bore the name Ahimelech.
Gill: David distributed the posterities of Eleazar and Ithamar into classes.

debates.question: Is the 'Ahimelech' who assists David the high priest Abiathar himself, or his son?

sides.position: Ahimelech is put for Abiathar, the high priest of David's day, who also bore the name Ahimelech
Ahimelech the son of Abiathar, a distinct man acting in the priestly business

schools.summary: Ellicott makes the heads of households, not individuals, the basis of division — 'heads of the men' vs 'heads of the fathers' (clans), cf. Josh. 7:14 — yielding sixteen courses for Eleazar and eight for Ithamar.
Gill: more chief men, heads of fathers' houses, were found among the sons of Eleazar than of Ithamar.

Ellicott: the two lines drew 'these with those' alternately to settle precedence in ministration (cf. Luke 1:8), the functions being the same for all; Keil grounds the lot in the fact that 'holy princes and princes of God' had come from both houses, so neither should have advantage.

Clarke: the lot prevented jealousies among equally noble families; Wesley: the disposal was 'of the Lord,' as Matthias was chosen by lot with prayer, and 'of God' means the things and ministrations of God (cf. 2 Chron. 19:11).
Gill: the lot fixed which course served first and who was chief, to prevent envy and strife over precedence, each serving a week.

themes: princes of God and of the sanctuary
the lot as the LORD's disposal

schools.summary: Keil gives the scene — Shemaiah the Levite scribe writing the classes before king and priests — wrestling with an obscure clause; Ellicott judges the text corrupt, restores 'one clan drawn for Eleazar and one for Ithamar' alternately (LXX 'one by one'), noting Ithamar's eight would be exhausted first (cf. 1 Chron. 25:22).

Clarke (with the Targum's 'Moses the great scribe') and Wesley trace the alternating draw — first Eleazar, then Ithamar, and so on until Ithamar's eight were spent and the rest fell to Eleazar's houses, double in number.
Gill names Shemaiah son of Nethaneel the scribe, with the Targum's identification of him as Moses.

Keil runs the whole roll of twenty-four and points to the summary of v.19; Ellicott links Jehoiarib and Jedaiah to 1 Chron. 9:10, notes the Maccabean princes sprang from Jehoiarib's house, and glosses 'came forth' as drawn from the urn (Josh. 16:1; 19:1).
Gill: the first lot fell to Jehoiarib, the other twenty-three to the rest in order.
Wesley: the lot 'came' out of the vessel in which all the lots were put and severally taken.
Ellicott reads Harim as 'flat-nosed' (cf. Latin Naso), recurring among returning priests at Ezra 2:39 and Neh. 3:11, and Seorim as 'barley,' i.e. 'bearded' (Latin Barbatus), found nowhere else.

Ellicott notes Malchijah recurs at Neh. 3:11, and reads Mijamin ('on the right hand') as perhaps a worn form equivalent to Benjamin, 'son of the right hand,' reappearing among the returned courses at Neh. 12:5.
Ellicott reads Hakkoz as 'the thorn' (cf. 1 Chron. 4:8) and notes that to the eighth course, Abijah (Gospel 'Abia'), belonged Zacharias, father of John the Baptist (Luke 1:5).

Matthew Henry makes the eighth course, Abijah, the proof that David's courses — though broken in bad reigns and by the captivity — continued in succession to the destruction of the second temple, since Zacharias served in it (Luke 1:5).

themes: the courses enduring to the Gospel

schools.summary: Ellicott spells Jeshuah as Hebrew Yeshua, Greek Iesous ('Jesus'), a form found only in Chronicles-Ezra-Nehemiah (cf. Ezra 2:2; Syriac/Arabic read 'Elisha'), and glosses Shecaniah (1 Chron. 3:21) as 'Iah is a neighbour.'

Ellicott renders Eliashib 'God will restore' (cf. 1 Chron. 3:24) and Jakim 'God/Jah will establish,' equivalent to Eliakim and Jecamiah (1 Chron. 8:19) — priestly house-names that are fragments of confession.
Ellicott reads Huppah as 'covering, canopy' (cf. Isa. 4:5; Huppim, 1 Chron. 7:12) and Joshebeab as 'May the Father cause to lead captive,' noting the LXX read it 'Eshbaal,' man of Baal.
Ellicott reads Bilgah 'smiling' (cf. Isaac the laugher; 'Bilgai' Neh. 10:9; Neh. 12:5) and Immer 'perhaps a lamb' (1 Chron. 9:10; Jer. 20:1) — names of gladness among the servants of the altar.

Ellicott renders Hezir 'hog' (Neh. 10:21; Syriac/Arabic soften to 'Ahaziah,' but Vulgate and LXX attest 'Hezir') and Aphses, Hebrew ha-pizzez, 'the scatterer,' found here only, with scattered versional spellings.
Ellicott renders Pethahiah 'Iah openeth,' i.e. sets free (cf. Jephthah; Ezra 10:23), and identifies Jehezekel as Hebrew Yehezqel — Ezekiel, 'God strengthens.'

themes: the courses as a litany of God's attributes

schools.summary: Ellicott reads Jachin 'He, Jah, setteth up, maketh firm' (same as Jehoiachin; Gen. 46:10; the temple pillar of 1 Kings 7:21) and Gamul 'weaned' (here only; cf. Isa. 11:8).

Ellicott renders Delaiah 'Jah draws out,' i.e. frees (1 Chron. 3:24; Jer. 36:12), and suspects Maaziah (here only) should be read 'Maadiah' (Neh. 12:5), recording the scatter of versional spellings that ends the roll.

Ellicott renders 'these were their classes for their service,' the rule 'ordained through Aaron' (Num. 2:1; 4:1), each course serving weekly from the sabbath (2 Kings 11:9; 2 Chron. 23:8), and cites Josephus that David's arrangements lasted to his own day.

Wesley: each course came into the temple every sabbath and served till the next, 'Aaron' meaning the high priest who bore his office and authority; Clarke: they followed the order Aaron laid down in his lifetime.
Gill: 'these were the orderings of them,' the classes into which they were divided.

Keil reads 'the rest' as the Levites left over after the priests, limited to those about the worship (singers/doorkeepers/treasurers enumerated separately in 1 Chron. 25-26); Ellicott notes the list begins with Kohathite heads, omitting the Gershonites (perhaps a lacuna), and identifies Shubael with Shebuel, Moses' grandson (1 Chron. 23:16).

Matthew Henry: the Levites cast lots 'over against their brethren, not their lords,' the priests not lording it over God's clergy (1 Pet. 5:3); elder and younger equal by lot, as in Christ no difference is made between elder and younger.
Gill: these were sons of Levi who were not priests but only Levites, most already named in 1 Chron. 23.

Keil connects Rehabiah's house with Eliezer, Moses' second son (1 Chron. 23:16), with Isshiah as head, and argues the new heads here (absent from ch. 23) prove ch. 23 gives fathers'-houses while this section gives official classes; Ellicott renders 'the chief was Isshiah,' the parallel (1 Chron. 23:17) saying only that Rehabiah's sons were very many.

Keil sets the Izharite house Shelomoth (1 Chron. 23:18) under the head Jahath — again absent from ch. 23, proving this is the register of classes; Ellicott notes the gentilic 'the Izharites' shows these proper names are names of houses or clans, not merely individuals.

Keil and Ellicott agree the Hebronite verse is mutilated — only the four house-names (from 1 Chron. 23:19) survive, not the heads of their classes; Bertheau would insert 'Hebron,' and Keil confesses it impossible to tell whether the heads were lost by a copyist or absent from the source.

themes: the missing heads and the interpreter's restraint

schools.summary: Keil traces Uzziel-Michah-Shamir and Michah's brother Isshiah with head Zechariah; Ellicott (cf. 1 Chron. 23:20, harmonising 'Jesiah'/'Isshiah') counts only five of nine Kohathite heads named, and both allow that a large house could form more than one class to reach the full twenty-four.

themes: houses forming more than one class

schools.summary: Ellicott: 'Beno' is Hebrew for 'his son,' not a name — read 'the sons of Jaaziah his son were Shoham, Zaccur, Ibri,' Jaaziah being a third son of Merari (cf. 1 Chron. 23:21); Keil transcribes 'Beno' as if a name in the list.
Clarke notes the remarkable silence — 'not a word is here spoken of the family of Gershom.'

debates.question: Is 'Beno' a proper name or the Hebrew for 'his son'?

sides.position: 'Beno' stands as a proper name among the sons of Merari
'Beno' is not a name but the Hebrew for 'his son' — read 'the sons of Jaaziah his son'

schools.summary: Ellicott defends Shoham ('onyx') and Ibri ('Hebrew') as genuine though unique, noting some pronounce vv.26-27 spurious 'against the evidence of the ancient versions'; Keil reads the closing lot as dividing all equally (Vulgate: the greater as the lesser), birth conferring no advantage.

Ellicott: Mahli's son Eleazar had no sons; his clan, being few, 'amalgamated with that of Kish-Jerahmeel' (cf. 1 Chron. 23:22); Keil notes that since Eleazar had no sons, only Jerahmeel (from Kish) is named as head of the class from Mahli's line.

Ellicott renders 'to Kish, the sons of Kish, Jerahmeel,' noting the plural 'sons of Kish' (as 1 Chron. 23:22) for a single heir; Keil makes Jerahmeel, through Kish, the head who carries Mahli's line after Eleazar failed of sons.

Ellicott notes the sons of Mushi (Mahli, Eder, Jerimoth, as 1 Chron. 23:23) are given as houses without their chiefs, and takes the subscription 'after the house of their fathers' to prove vv.20-30 once held a complete catalogue of Levitical clans, its present defects perhaps original, perhaps transcriptional; Keil closes the Merarite branch with the same names.

Ellicott renders 'over against their brethren' as 'just like their brethren the priests' (cf. 1 Chron. 26:12; 2 Sam. 16:13), argues the list covers only the Levites who assisted the priests (the 24,000 of 1 Chron. 23:4, hence the omitted Gershonites), and thinks the classes were probably twenty-four by analogy with 1 Chron. 25.

Clarke: the Levites in twenty-four orders were appointed by lot to serve under the twenty-four orders of priests, elder and younger placed by lot not seniority (Bishop Patrick), adding 'we are very little interested in the subject.'
Gill: the Levites, divided into twenty-four classes, cast lots to decide which should serve alongside each priestly course, in the same solemn public manner as the priests.

themes: the Levites cast lots like the priests
elder and younger equal by lot
the LORD's disposing of the whole service
